summary: This lesson introduces prepositions as a word class, teaching that after prepositions Spanish uses subject pronouns (con él, para ella, de nosotros) except for 'me' and 'you informal' which become mí and ti. It also introduces decir (to say/tell), the distinction between le replacing prepositions vs using explicit prepositions, and ser vs estar with prepositions.

teachings.summary: After prepositions (con, para, de, etc.), Spanish uses subject pronouns: con él, para ella, de nosotros, con ustedes. The exceptions are 'me' which becomes mí and 'you informal' which becomes ti after prepositions.
teachings.rule: After prepositions: use él/ella/ellos/ellas/nosotros/nosotras/usted/ustedes. Exceptions: me -> mí, te -> ti. So: para mí, de ti, con él, de nosotros.
teachings.notes: Prepositions show movement or position. 'Es de ustedes' and 'es para ustedes' use ser (characteristic of the object), but 'está con ustedes' uses estar (location = state).

teachings.summary: Le and les effectively replace prepositions like 'to', 'for', 'from' in everyday Spanish. Instead of 'compro para ellos' you say 'les compro'. This makes Spanish use fewer prepositions than English. The meaning (to/for/from) is understood from context.
teachings.rule: In everyday speech, use le/les instead of para él/de él etc. 'Me lo compra' can mean 'he buys it for me' OR 'he buys it from me' - context clarifies.

examples.english: I buy for them. (= compro para ellos)

teachings.notes: Spanish uses fewer prepositions than English. You'll naturally start shedding prepositions as you listen more to Spanish.

teachings.summary: Decir means to say or to tell, as distinct from hablar (to speak/talk). Decir is an -ir verb.
Reviewed hacer (to do/make). hace = he/she does. Distinguished from ha (haber). Practiced with 'hacer un favor'.
teachings.notes: Common confusion: ha (from haber) vs hace (from hacer).
